proof_stages.label: Recursive positive-geometry setup
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines a positive geometry as a real semi-algebraic region in a variety whose unique rational top form has only logarithmic boundary poles and residues equal to canonical forms of boundary positive geometries.

proof_stages.label: Geometric questions from canonical forms
proof_stages.summary: The speaker asks whether positive geometries admit smooth simple-normal-crossing log resolutions and whether their canonical forms encode de Rham cohomology, with the latter question leading to hyperplane arrangement complements.

proof_stages.label: Realisable arrangement basis heuristic
proof_stages.summary: For a real full-rank matrix defining a projective hyperplane arrangement, known Orlik-Solomon/Ziegler numerology identifies top cohomology dimension with bounded regions, and residue induction shows that the canonical forms of those bounded regions form a basis in the realizable case.

proof_stages.label: Oriented-matroid and Orlik-Solomon dictionary
proof_stages.summary: To formulate the statement combinatorially, the talk introduces chirotopes, realizability, the reduced Orlik-Solomon algebra, topes, deletion/contraction, and the combinatorial residue map, while warning that nonrealizable oriented matroids can defeat naive geometric deformation arguments.

proof_stages.label: Canonical forms for arbitrary topes
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem assigns uniquely to every tope of every oriented matroid an element of the reduced Orlik-Solomon algebra characterized by rank-one normalization and contraction-residue recursion, and it gives a triangulation formula whose independence is proved by nested induction.

proof_stages.label: Bounded-tope bases and consequences
proof_stages.summary: For any general extension playing the role of a hyperplane at infinity, canonical forms of bounded topes form a basis of the top Orlik-Solomon degree and, after truncation, of the whole algebra, connecting to chamber bases, model complexes, pairings, and future multiplication-constant formulas.
